skill.name: css-development
skill.description: Guides CSS development workflows for semantic component classes, Tailwind @apply composition, dark mode coverage, refactoring, validation, and test planning.
skill.summary: Create, refactor, and validate maintainable CSS component patterns.
skill.author: 2389-research

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and medium hits, but review found they are primarily Markdown backticks, CSS examples, checklist text, and local test snippets. No prompt injection, malicious network behavior, credential access, obfuscation, or destructive filesystem behavior was found. Remaining risk is limited to normal coding-workflow guidance that may run project-local tests and read local CSS files.

low_findings.title: Static command execution hits are mostly Markdown syntax false positives
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged many inline backticks and fenced examples as Ruby or shell execution. Reviewed locations describe CSS class names, Tailwind @apply usage, checklists, and documentation examples rather than executable skill code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are Markdown prose and CSS terminology, not executable code. Similar static hits throughout the files follow the same documentation pattern.

low_findings.title: Project-local test commands may execute repository scripts
low_findings.description: The skill recommends commands such as npm test and vitest while creating or refactoring CSS. This is normal for a coding skill, but these commands execute the target project test environment and should be used only in trusted workspaces.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command text is explicit and legitimate for CSS validation. Risk depends on the project being audited, not on malicious behavior in the skill files.

low_findings.title: Filesystem examples are limited to local CSS test reads and edits
low_findings.description: The filesystem hits are examples or workflow steps that read styles/components.css, add CSS tests, or edit component styles. No evidence found for secret harvesting, broad filesystem traversal, or destructive file operations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples read local CSS files for tests and validation. The reviewed context does not show access to credentials, hidden directories, or unrelated user data.

low_findings.title: Weak crypto and network reconnaissance hits are dismissed after context review
low_findings.description: High static hits for weak cryptography and low hits for reconnaissance did not correspond to cryptographic APIs, network calls, ports, hosts, or scanning behavior. Reviewed lines are skill descriptions, CSS workflow text, or markup examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious categories are not semantically present in the reviewed content. The cited lines contain CSS guidance or report text, not network or cryptographic operations.

content.value_statement: CSS work often becomes inconsistent across components, tests, and dark mode states. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through structured CSS creation, validation, and refactoring workflows.
actual_capabilities: Routes CSS requests to create, validate, or refactor workflows.
Promotes semantic class naming for reusable component styles.
Guides Tailwind @apply composition with dark mode variants.
Provides checklists for CSS tests and component rendering tests.
Reviews CSS for naming, documentation, composition, and coverage issues.
Supports framework-agnostic examples for React, Vue, and HTML usage.
limitations: Does not include executable automation or dedicated CSS parser code.
Requires the assistant to inspect the target project files before making changes.
Test commands depend on the project toolchain and may need adjustment.
Visual correctness still needs human or screenshot-based review for complex UI.

use_cases.title: Create Reusable UI Styles
use_cases.description: Add semantic component classes with Tailwind composition, dark mode states, documentation, and focused tests.

use_cases.title: Review CSS Before Merge
use_cases.description: Check naming, @apply usage, dark mode coverage, test coverage, and documentation before shipping style changes.

use_cases.title: Refactor Utility-Heavy Markup
use_cases.description: Convert repeated inline styles or utility groups into maintainable semantic classes while preserving behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Button Style
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the CSS development skill to create a primary button style with dark mode support and a simple test plan.

prompt_templates.title: Validate Existing Styles
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my components.css file for semantic naming, Tailwind @apply usage, dark mode coverage, and missing tests.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor Repeated Utilities
prompt_templates.prompt: Find repeated Tailwind utility combinations in these components and suggest semantic CSS classes that preserve the current UI.

prompt_templates.title: Migrate a Component Set
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor this component set into semantic CSS patterns, update markup usage, add coverage checks, and summarize migration impact.

output_examples.input: Create a new alert component style for success and error states.
output_examples.output: A structured plan with semantic class names, dark mode requirements, usage notes, and tests that verify each alert variant.
A validation report listing naming issues, missing dark mode states, composition opportunities, test gaps, and prioritized fixes.

output_examples.input: Refactor repeated Tailwind utilities in several React components.
output_examples.output: A migration summary describing extracted classes, updated files, preserved custom classes, verification steps, and remaining follow-up work.

best_practices: Inspect existing CSS patterns before adding new component classes.
Include dark mode variants for colored, bordered, and interactive elements.
Pair semantic CSS changes with static CSS checks and rendering tests.
anti_patterns: Creating new classes before checking whether existing classes can compose the design.
Using visual names like btn-blue when the class should describe purpose.
Running project test commands without understanding the repository toolchain.

faq.question: Does this skill write CSS automatically?
faq.answer: It provides workflows and examples. The assistant still needs to inspect and edit the target project files.

faq.question: Does it require Tailwind CSS?
faq.answer: The guidance is built around Tailwind @apply patterns, but naming and review principles can help other CSS projects.

faq.question: Can it handle dark mode?
faq.answer: Yes. Each workflow asks for dark mode variants on colored, bordered, and interactive styles.

faq.question: Can it review existing CSS?
faq.answer: Yes. The validate workflow checks naming, @apply usage, dark mode coverage, tests, and documentation.

faq.question: Is this safe to use in any repository?
faq.answer: Use normal caution. The skill may suggest local test commands, so only run them in repositories you trust.

faq.question: Does it include visual regression testing?
faq.answer: It recommends visual inspection and screenshot comparison when available, but it does not provide a dedicated visual test runner.
